data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The simultaneous collapse resulted from a synchronized climatic shock (the 3.2ka event) that specifically targeted the rain-fed agricultural hinterlands of the Northern Levant and Anatolia, causing multi-year famine that delegitimized the divine kingship of palace centers while Egypt survived due to its reliance on predictable Nile flood irrigation.", "mechanism": "Exogenous climatic forcing causing agricultural failure and subsequent political legitimacy crisis", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Mycenaean Greece", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Nile Delta regions"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data showing normal precipitation levels in the Eastern Mediterranean between 1200-1150 BC", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Prioritizes the environmental determinism angle, explicitly contrasting the rain-fed vs. irrigation-fed agricultural systems to explain the differential survival of Egypt."}
